title: Vanilla fudge
subtitle: Classic British set fudge with a grainy, crumbling texture
excerpt: Vanilla fudge is a cooked sugar confection made from sugar, butter, and condensed milk, boiled to soft-ball stage and beaten as it cools until it turns thick, pale, and grainy. The texture should be firm enough to cut cleanly but crumble on the tongue rather than chewy like toffee. A sugar thermometer is necessary.
sourceNotes: Fudge is an American and British confectionery tradition documented from the late nineteenth century. The beaten-grainy texture is produced by controlled crystallisation as the sugar mixture cools. British condensed-milk fudge is a twentieth-century variant documented in British domestic cookery manuals.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut squares in an airtight tin. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: soft-ball stage, 116°C on the sugar thermometer

content.text: Vanilla fudge is boiled sugar, butter, and condensed milk taken to 116°C and beaten as it cools until it thickens and turns opaque. The beating is what makes fudge fudge; it breaks down the sugar crystals into a fine, grainy texture that is quite different from the smooth chewiness of a toffee or caramel. It needs no oven, but it does need a sugar thermometer and attention while it cooks.

Sugar cooked above 100°C causes serious burns. Keep children away from the hob while making fudge. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

items.prepNote: add at the end, off the heat

content.text: Combine all ingredients except the vanilla in a large, heavy saucepan over low heat. Stir continuously until the sugar has fully dissolved; the mixture should be smooth with no grittiness when you rub a little between your fingers. Increase the heat to medium and clip a sugar thermometer to the side of the pan with the tip not touching the base. Cook, stirring frequently, until the mixture reaches 116°C (soft-ball stage). This takes about 20 to 25 minutes from when you raise the heat.

Remove from the heat and stir in the vanilla. Pour into a large bowl or beat directly in the pan. Leave to cool undisturbed for 5 minutes. Then beat vigorously with a wooden spoon or electric hand mixer on low speed. The mixture will start to lose its gloss, thicken, and become paler as the sugar crystallises. Keep beating (it can take 10 to 15 minutes by hand) until the fudge is very thick, opaque, and holds its shape when you drop a spoonful back into the bowl.

Pour into a buttered and lined 20 cm square tin. Smooth the top and leave at room temperature for at least 2 hours until fully set and firm. Cut into squares with a sharp knife. If the fudge is too firm to cut cleanly, let it come to room temperature for 10 minutes first.
